text: Pakistan's monsoon season runs from late June to September. The Arabian Sea branch brings heavy rainfall to Sindh and southern Punjab, while the Bay of Bengal branch affects northern Punjab and KPK. Karachi typically receives 6080% of its annual rainfall in JulyAugust.

Monsoon onset over Pakistan is declared when the 850 hPa wind over Karachi shifts from westerly to southwesterly. In 2022 a super-flood year, monsoon rainfall exceeded 300% of normal in Balochistan and Sindh, inundating one-third of the country.
Pre-monsoon thunderstorms, called nor'westers locally, are common in Punjab during AprilMay. These squall lines produce gusty winds exceeding 70 km/h and hail, often lasting 12 hours.

Lahore experiences severe smog from October to February, driven by crop residue burning in surrounding districts, vehicular emissions, and brick kilns. AQI values exceeding 500 (Hazardous) are recorded on peak days, with visibility dropping below 200 metres.

The Lahore smog season is worsened by thermal inversions that trap pollutants near the surface. Schools are often closed when PM2.5 exceeds 300 µg/m³. The primary agricultural burning window is October 15  November 20.

Karachi is vulnerable to Arabian Sea cyclones, historically most active in MayJune (pre-monsoon) and OctoberNovember (post-monsoon). Cyclone Biparjoy in June 2023 was one of the longest-lived Arabian Sea cyclones on record, making landfall near the SindhGujarat border.

Arabian Sea cyclones intensify rapidly when sea surface temperatures exceed 28°C. Karachi's coastal geography provides limited lead time  surge warnings are issued when a storm is within 300 km of the coast.

Karachi experiences deadly heat waves in MayJune before monsoon onset. The June 2015 heat wave killed over 1,200 people, with temperatures exceeding 45°C and high humidity preventing effective cooling. Loo winds from the desert amplify heat stress.

Quetta, at 1,680 m elevation, experiences harsh winters with temperatures regularly dropping below -10°C from December to February. Snowfall is common on the surrounding Chiltan and Zarghoon ranges. The city receives most of its annual precipitation as winter snow.
Balochistan's cold winters are driven by continental air masses from Central Asia. Quetta's airport has recorded -18°C. Roads to Ziarat and Ziarat are frequently closed due to snowdrifts in January.

Islamabad receives bimodal rainfall  a winter-spring peak (JanuaryMarch) from western disturbances and a summer monsoon peak (JulyAugust). Annual rainfall averages 1,142 mm, making it one of the wettest major Pakistani cities.
Islamabad and Rawalpindi are prone to hailstorms in MarchApril associated with westerly wave troughs. Hailstones up to 3 cm diameter have damaged vehicles and crops in the Potohar plateau region.
Pakistan holds one of the world's highest reliably recorded temperatures: 53.7°C measured at Turbat, Balochistan, on 28 May 2017. This is the highest reliably recorded temperature in Asia.

Jacobabad and Sibi in Sindh regularly record temperatures above 50°C in MayJune. Wet-bulb temperatures in Jacobabad have exceeded the survivability threshold of 35°C wet-bulb during peak heat events.

The Loo is a hot, dry westerly or north-westerly wind that blows across the Indo-Gangetic plain and Pakistan's Punjab in summer. Wind speeds reach 3050 km/h and temperatures can rise 58°C above ambient. It desiccates standing crops and causes dehydration.

Haboobs are massive dust storms common in the Thar Desert and Cholistan in MayJune. They form ahead of squall lines when outflow winds loft dust to heights of 13 km. Visibility drops to zero and they can travel 200300 km.

Western disturbances are extratropical cyclones originating over the Mediterranean that bring winter rainfall and snowfall to Pakistan's north. They reach Pakistan as upper-level troughs, causing widespread cloud cover and rainfall in Punjab, KPK, and AJK between November and March.

Peshawar has a semi-arid climate with hot summers exceeding 40°C in June. The surrounding Khyber hills channel afternoon thunderstorms from the southwest during monsoon months. The valley traps heat and the city often records Pakistan's highest temperatures in May.

Pakistan's kharif (summer) crop season runs from April to October. Key crops include rice, cotton, sugarcane, and maize. Monsoon onset and intensity directly determine kharif yields  delayed onset by more than 2 weeks historically reduces rice output by 1520%.

The rabi (winter) crop season runs October to April. Wheat is the dominant rabi crop. Winter rainfall from western disturbances is critical  a dry NovemberJanuary reduces wheat yields significantly. Fog in Punjab can cause fungal disease in standing wheat.

Dense radiation fog blankets Lahore, Faisalabad, and the GT Road corridor from December to February. Visibility falls below 50 metres on peak days, causing motorway closures and flight delays. The fog forms overnight when humidity is high and winds are calm after monsoon soil moisture.

Cumulonimbus clouds (Cb) are the primary producers of heavy rain and hail in Pakistan. Their anvil tops often reach the tropopause at 1618 km during monsoon. Cb development in the afternoon is triggered by surface heating over the Punjab plains.

Stratocumulus sheets are common over Karachi in winter and pre-monsoon, driven by marine air from the Arabian Sea. They produce little rain but maintain overcast skies for days. Morning fog on the coast often burns off by 10 AM.

Pakistan receives intense UV radiation year-round due to its low latitude and high altitude in northern areas. Peak UV Index values of 11+ (extreme) are recorded in Karachi and Islamabad from April to August. UV exposure risk is highest between 10 AM and 3 PM.

Sea breezes are critical to Karachi's livability in summer, keeping coastal temperatures 35°C cooler than inland Sindh. The breeze sets in around noon and peaks in late afternoon. On days when the sea breeze fails, Karachi can record temperatures above 44°C.

Topi, in the Swabi district of KPK, has a semi-arid to sub-humid climate. It receives 500700 mm annual rainfall, most of it during monsoon. Summer temperatures reach 42°C but winters are mild. The nearby Indus River moderates humidity.

Pakistan is among the top 10 countries most vulnerable to climate change. Glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs) from the Karakoram and Hindu Kush have increased. The 2022 super-floods were attributed partly to climate-amplified monsoon extremes.

Pakistan's major cities face severe air quality challenges. Lahore frequently ranks among the world's most polluted cities in NovemberDecember. Main pollutants are PM2.5 from biomass burning, vehicle exhaust, and industrial emissions. AQI above 300 triggers school closures.

Karachi's air quality is impacted by vehicle emissions, the Kemari port industrial zone, and seasonal dust. AQI spikes occur during southwest monsoon failure years when dust from Balochistan blows in. Marine aerosols from the Arabian Sea can mask true pollution readings.

Pakistan recorded 366 mm of rain in Islamabad in a single day in July 2001  a national record for a 24-hour period in a major city. Urban flooding in Islamabad and Rawalpindi occurs when the Nullah Lai overflows during intense monsoon rainfall events.
